title: 2025/07/16 - MA
displayTitle: 2025-07-16 - Māori Affairs Committee - Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework)
topic: Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework)
description: 16 July 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework) Te Puni Kōkiri Dave Samuels, Secretary for Māori Development Paula Rawiri, Deputy Secretary, Policy Bernard Te Paa, General Manager, Policy – Wellbeing Terina Cowan, Deputy Secretary, Strategy Manaia King, Deputy Secretary, Corporate Grace Smit, Deputy Secretary, Regions Steve Sutton, Deputy Secretary, Governance Rebecca Nesus, Manager, Māori Public Policy Hannah James, Principal Advisor, Māori Public Policy
descriptionSummary: Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework). Te Puni Kōkiri. Dave Samuels, Secretary for Māori Development

chunks.text: Okay. Pilot, shoot, pilot for the presentation.

Um, thank you very much, um, Dave and your team for coming before us. We're pretty excited actually. This has obviously been a piece of work that's been, uh, been going for quite some time from, uh, what we've read. And we're really keen to, uh, get an understanding of where you've got to and, and what we've got here to be presented to us today. So I'll, I'll let you open up with some instructory Comments. Thank you. Uh, thank you, Mr. Chair.

And, um, greetings again to the committee members. You spend a lot of time here. Yeah, but that's a good thing. Um, look, um, I'm joined here by my executive leadership team, but also, um, some of the subject matter experts to talk through the Kopa that we are about to discuss today, starting with, and then of course, the settlements, uh, commitments.

But before we start, I, I just wanna frame the role that Ty plays in terms of policy advice, and in particular, the anchor points and the foundation upon which we carry out our role as the lead policy advisor on Maori development and the relationships between Iwi, Maori and the Crown.

And it's important that we reflect on those anchor points. 'cause I do know that, um, you have asked a number of times because the previous strategic intentions document finished, um, in June, and you asked a number of times, where are we going to from here? Um, I do have that document.

It was tabled in, um, on the 1st of July, our strategic contentions document. But I just wanna highlight the anchor points. The first is our act, the Maori Development Act. And under section five of that act, and I won't go through the all of the wording, but it's important to note, there are two areas there.

One is monitoring, monitoring other government departments and agencies. And the second is around promoting increases in the levels of achievement of Maori. So there's those two things. In fact, they go in the opposite order, increasing levels of achievement and monitoring.

So that's our first act, um, anchor point. And it's a statutory, the, it's a statutory anchor, anchor point in legislation. The second are the cabinet papers that the minister took through cabinet last August. And as part of that, he sought to clarify the role of ti and also transfer functions from to ti.

And so that there would be one strong voice within the public service to advise on those issues with regards to Maori development and, um, iwi Maori relationships with the crowd. Now, that has led to five key functions that we have. The first is to promote increases in levels of Maori achievement in education, training, employment, health and economic resource development.

That's under the act, and it's also reinforced by the cabinet paper. The second is to monitor and liaise with departments to ensure the services they provide to or for Maori are adequate. That's under the act and under the two cabinet papers. The third is to advise the government and the public service on the distinct rights, interests and responsibilities of Hapu Iwi and Maori.

That's under the cabinet paper. And the fourth is to lead post settlement relationships with PSGs and monitor the crown's implementation of treaty settlement commitments. That's under the cabinet papers. And the fifth is the support investment into wau centered, locally led government enabled initiatives.
And we've briefed you on the flagship example of that, which is Wau Water. Yep. And the final anchor point, um, is our strategic contentions document. And if I could just pass those around.
And in particular, um, just draw your attention to page five of that. And it's our strategy on a, on a page.

Page. Which page? Page five. Yeah. In, in, oh, page, page 10. Page 10. Strategy on the page. That's a lovely photo of I'll purpose this wise. Yeah, look at me. So, um, page five. Now that, that highlights our vision, our purpose, our role. It's a little different from our previous strategy in that it identifies our two approaches.

One is to that we are about to speak to you today, that, and the second is our wau centered locally led government enabled. One is our policy approach, the other is our delivery approach. And then we have our five strategic priority areas underneath that. And you can see what those are.

And of course, it has our values underneath the, the purpose of the strategy is so that our staff see line of sight between what they do and what our vision is. Thriving wau, what our purpose is to build an New Zealand. But Wau can all stand, thrive and belong.

And we do that by drawing strength from our past, because we've been around for a long, long time, initially in 1840, the first iteration of the protector department. Then there is the role, which has changed because of those cabinet papers in August, which includes Maori development as it was always, um, our role, but now also the, the relationship between Iwi, Maori and the Crown.

And then we have our two strategic, um, approaches, policy and delivery, and then our five strategic priorities. And underneath that are our values. So, so that, that is the strategy, and those are the three anchor points, the statutory functions, the cabinet paper papers, and then this, the strategic contentions document.

So that brings us to why we are here this morning. To total means to listen, consider, and analyze deliberately before speaking, writing, and advising. And essentially what that means is being thoughtful and considered before you take action.

And as a, uh, policy ministry, we take this seriously to think very carefully about how we think about the treaty relationship between Iwi Maori and the crowd, and how we go through problem solving and policy issues. So that's just my introduction.

Um, I have Paula, my deputy secretary, and Bernard here as one of the general managers to talk to more of the detail around this. So I just wonder if we, um, quickly go into a presentation that, that Paula and Bernard will carry out. We'll keep this short. It's about 15 minutes, including Mike Oral Max.

And questions? No, uh, please ask questions throughout the presentation. Is that all right, Mr. Chair? Yeah. Thanks, Doug. Yep. So You're happy to have questions during the presentation, or you mean afterwards? Okay. We've just got, um, four, four slides. Um, oh, cool. So, um,

um, I wanted to, uh, go to the next slide. Um, and following on from, uh, Dave's, it's important to locate this discussion within the context of our strategic contentions for two reasons. Really. Policy is a critical lever, uh, for government in order to be able to deliver, uh, better outcomes for and with Whanau, iwi and Maori.

And secondly, as Dave has touched on, we think by naming our policy approach with being overt in both our policy standards, what we expect, but also how we conduct ourselves. And it touches on the part that Dave talked about with respect to considering being thoughtful about how we listen, how we analyze before we take action.

So together with our delivery approach, these two, um, elements coming together can we think, can help us to undertake our role as the government's lead policy advisor on Maori development and Iwi and Maori, um, related with, um, we know that there are many tools that policy advisors across government use, and we want to acknowledge these, um, the, they're important guidance, um, both to help support capability within the public service, but also to be able to apply across, um, our policy advice.

However, with te we think that there's a distinguishing element, um, that we are calling out, which is that, uh, TE is explicitly grounded in the relationship that's been established between Hawi and the Crown, uh, through the te, uh, through TE the of wa.

So our goal is to, as a key public policy framework, and in doing so, we think that the public service and decision makers will have a single point of reference to deliver more effective public services and improved outcomes for whanau, iwi and Maori.

This presentation is quite a milestone. Um, as we begin to take our next steps forward, um, like is always good practice, it's important to look back. Uh hmm. And as such, I do want to acknowledge the previous work that's been undertaken without which we would not be here today to talk to you about this.

Uh, this. And I particularly want to, uh, acknowledge in April, 1997, uh, along with the, at the time Honorable Justice eth. And at the time, Joe Williams presented a treaty framework seminar to the New Zealand Law Society, which framed the Treaty of Wang as a policy, uh, framework.

And this is, I think, an important, um, piece of information that can, that has helped us. Uh, secondly, in 1999, this concept was refined further and became Tuko Treaty framework for which to support the policy development to identify treaty implications in, in our work.

At that time, in 2001, uh, which is still in publication and circulation today, uh, a guide to the Treaty of Ang is expressed by the courts in the Ang Tribunal, was another key, um, information document that provided that, that we've based this work on.

Um, circa 2004 to 2010, you may all be familiar with the Maori potential approach that shifted the framework, um, towards the realization of Maori potential. And I think of, um, uh, honorable at this time, who was quite, um, an avid fan for realizing Maori potential at the time.

I did try and look for a cup this morning to see if we had some collateral around it, but couldn't find it. Um, between 2011 and 2016, agency started to develop their own, um, expressions of this work. And I would like to acknowledge the Ministry of Education developed Pu Moana, which was about, um, excellent relationships between the Ministry of Education and Iwi at the time, again, calling out how they intend to work.

Um, and then, um, uh, treasuries Wai order is another important tool, uh, that looks to help drive wellbeing from a Maori perspective. So those are important pieces of information that have helped us to come to where we are today. Um, so fast forward to 2020, and through a self-initiated performance improvement review, the need to develop a new Maori macro policy framework was highlighted, and this was expected to help the organization focus our efforts in terms of our performance.

And it was, at this time, not long after that, that formally adopted uni, uh, te or, um, we entered in, and that was as our, as our way of doing things. And we entered into a memorandum of understanding with, to develop a guidance document on this led to the delivery of a series of workshops, uh, with which included workshops with public sector leaders, uh, testing and trialing the guidance with, uh, we've had reviews from the treaty, uh, treaty provisions overview group from DMC's Policy Project, the Leadership Development Center at, and so,

and we've made further refinements as we've gone along. So we're actually quite proud to bring this and be able to present this to you today. And it seems kind of, it seems appropriate, um, that this is kind of the, the, that this is an important milestone, as I've said, um, to be able to present this to you today. Um, it's a rii based framework.

Uh, it helps us explore and consider the collective and individual rights, interests, and responsibilities of au Hapu Iwi Maori. And it provides public servants with a consistent approach to the application of across a range and engagement. And I'm gonna hand it to burn to take us into a deep dive into the, Uh, in our memo, the committee name, uh, in introducing the framework and key concept con concepts of, uh, as Dave has referred to previously, um, uh, means to listen, consider, and analyze deliberately

before speaking, writing, and advising. In practical terms, analysis begins with defining, clarifying and testing the issue or opportunity at hand. Essentially, what is the, it this aligns with the first step of the policy lifecycle, defining the problem or creating the opportunity.

From this, there are two cut through questions that we need to ask. What is the specific, clearly defined, commonly agreed question? What is the IT you are trying to solve? And who is the IT for? The key concepts of are discussed on pages seven and eight of the guidance documents you have before you to summarize, DE has four representative communities identified in the corners of the framework, they being ua, PU citizens, and the crown, the four axes of de, uh, on the perimeter, uh, of the diagram, uh,

the partnership axi, which reflects the relationship established through the signing of teti, the treaty where parties act reasonably honorably and in good faith. The kawana aspect or access, whose function is to acknowledge the role of the government to run the country and make day-to-day decisions.

Access, which recognizes that there are matters that only, that's only in the domain of Iwi and Hapu, acknowledging their preexisting rights prior to the signing of te the treaty. And finally, the ANA access, which recognizes Maori as Tua while establishing the concept of citizenship, thereby recognizing Maori as citizens.

In totality, the four axis, uh, recognize the individual and connective rights, interests and responsibilities of whanau, hapu, iwi, and Maori. The four elements provide guidance, uh, for working across each of the associated axis, uh, with tko describing the desired outcome for the framework.

So what is the usability o of across the public service? Teo is designed to ensure public policy decisions deliberately consider the individual and collective rights of iwi and Maori. For the users of Teo, the perspective is clear for public servants.

Uh, it will support policy decision makers to consistently give effect to tei the treaty, enabling policy makers and decision makers to understand who is impacted and to what extent. Thereby delivering more efficient public services and improved outcomes for ministers.

Uh, as the application of extends across the public service, s ministers would expect to receive advice based on the framework that is more consistent in its quality and support, supportive of better decision making. For members of Parliament, Ong could support a better understanding of the perspectives of constituents.

Mm-hmm. Especially engaging with them in their multiple roles as citizens, community representatives, or hapu or Iwi members. And finally, for use yourselves as select committee members from across parliament, you play a key role in the deme democratic process through your work. In considering bills, scrutinizing government finances and activity, and engaging in with the public, the framework provides a consistent point of reference that will help in your inquiries and in understanding the rights, interests and responsibilities of citizens of New Zealand.

Some of the anticipated impacts and outcomes of applying the framework would be that by embedding the framework, agencies will be better positioned to consider Maori perspectives to key issues. This will contribute towards increased public sector confidence in applying te TEI or treaty analysis in developing robust policy advice, improving engagement of government agencies with Iwi and Hapu, Tua and Maori, and increased quality of policy advice to ministers across the agencies were implementing into their strategy, policy, and engagement process.

So what's next for, there are three carriers, um, of focus in the implementation of this framework. Firstly, for tools and guidance, has been working on ways to apply this framework in all of our own advice. This has included updating our official correspondence templates and incorporating into our second opinion advice.

Essentially, we see our role to role model this framework, uh, to build this. Officials are developing learning modules, case study exemplars, quality assurance, and monitoring frameworks to help build capacity across the public sector. Officials are delivering workshops on te, which will cover the foundations and application in practice from an engagement and implementation perspective.

We are piloting, uh, implementation for the remainder of 2025 to embed TE as a key strategy, policy and engagement framework across the following agencies, the Ministry of Justice, department of Corrections, ministry of Culture and Heritage, department of Conservation, and the Ministry of Education with broader public service implementation to AC implementation to occur from 2026, it is important to emphasize that this is an active learning module of implementation.

We are embarking on starting with a focus on those willing to participate and inserting feedback loops, uh, as people's practice, uh, practice its application. Finally, in terms of mentoring, uh, and monitoring, TE's Strengthened mentoring and mon monitoring role provides us with the opportunity to work with agencies in two ways.

When agencies adopt te, uh, TE will provide mentorship to support the appropriate adoption and application of the framework. And from a monitoring perspective, in the application of across an agency's mahi, we would expect to see policy advice that is fit for purpose, delivering to the distinct needs of those for whom it is designed.

Uh, and we hand it back to the committee for any questions at this time. Thank you, maam. Kiara, um, Mr. We'll open up for any questions of anybody at this stage. Greg, do you wanna Start? Yeah. So, um, thank you so much for the, the amount of work that has gone for this, uh, and I appreciate, appreciate the, um, the gestation period of this.

Um, could you, I, um, read the document carefully. You stand and listen carefully today, and I, I'm still, I'm still struggling to land it practically, so I love the, um, the vibe, your Honor. Um, uh, could you give us a couple of examples perhaps of how you, now that it's been implemented and landed, how you could see, just talk us through the process.

So, Bernard, you mentioned, um, it's gonna roll out the Department of Corrections Mystery of Justice. Could you perhaps take a couple of pieces of work that you know, are, are underway or coming up, and then talk us through how that work, how that process is gonna be different because of the application of this? Can I Yeah. Jump in there with an example of what is the problem?

Yes, please. Yeah, yeah. Um, in here, and Bernard talked about roles and representation and on the access Yeah. UA and Maori citizens. Yes. Well, part of the problem that public servants are confronted with when they're engaging with Maori is who are they engaging with?

And for what purpose? Are you engaging with a citizen or are you engaging with tanga? Yeah. And as a representative or as a, uh, constituency of an iwi or hapu. And unless you're clear about that, it can lead to, are you engaging with Maori as a treaty partner?

Yeah. Or are you engaging with Maori as citizens of New Zealand? Now I know from my own experience arriving, um, back in New Zealand in 2019, what struck me was there were a number of advisory groups, what I would call advisory groups with various names that were in government departments, that the government departments assumed incorrectly.

They were the treaty partner. And they, So say that last part again, Assumed incorrectly that they were the treaty partner. Oh. So the government departments are dealing with this group think, assuming, mis assuming that, that they're the treaty partner. Okay. That's Right. Yeah. And those groups also believing that assumption, right?

They were appointed by the crown. The treaty partner is not appointed by the crown. Sure. This framework makes it clear who are you dealing with and for what purpose. You're dealing with individual Maori citizens who are providing you with advice in that example.

Right. And it could be a, you know, and that advice would come through multiple experiences of being Maori on that access. But Right. Then they are advisors, they're advising you, you are the crown, you are representatives of representatives of the crown seeking advice.

Ah, however, if you are wanting to speak to the treaty partner, then look at the partnership access, be clear about who you're speaking to and for what purpose. Ah, gosh. That is, that's fascinating because that has implications across so many parts of the government.

But if we confuse this, not only do we undermine the partnership because it's the wrong people. Yeah. Not every Maori is speaking as the treaty partner all the time. I'm an example of that right now. I, so, so, so that is, is part of the problem of, of being clear about who you are speaking to and what situation and for what purpose.
Um, wow. It's a completely different, yeah. Yes it is. And um, and I'm not quite sure how we got to that point. Yeah. Uh, but, but that, that actually happened in a, uh, a number of times.

So thinking about this as a tool for the public service, um, to build on Dave's point, public servants are not the treaty partner, and they're not an expression of the treaty partner. Uh, so what this tool asks public servants to do, and the development of policy, uh, advice Mm.

Um, to government is, uh, consider, uh, well listen to and consider and analyze what a particular problem is that they're trying to solve, or what is the opportunity that they're trying to, uh, enable. And by starting in, in that position, what it asks the public service to do is locate, uh, we're clear.

We, we, we, we work, we're part of the katanga, we are on the KA a access. Yeah. We, we don't represent Tonga. Yeah. Um, and so forth. So we start in the KAA space. Then you need to locate, well, what is the problem you are trying to resolve? Um, or the opportunity you are trying to create.

And, um, and then take you through a process which looks to be, start to be very clear about, uh, about that defining the problem. And as Burna said, and for who, um, if you take an example, uh, for instance around, um, housing after the weather, housing crisis, after the, the extreme weather events a couple of years ago, um, if you just take that as a broad, um, uh, a broad statement, you then need to understand, well, which housing crisis are you talking about in relation to everything that happened, um, along the East Coast

and up in Tamaki? Um, is it land that is, is it in relation to UA or land, uh, that is UA Maori, uh, is it in relation to land that is held by general, you know, freehold land? Um, is it the housing crisis related to people's homes that were affected by the site phone?

And once you start, 'cause it can come from, it can be all of that. And so as part of a policy process, what you're asking, um, the pe people who are working on that to do is identify what is the issue you are trying to solve and who are the people that are affected by that? Because by doing that, it could lead you to both who you need to engage with and the different roles and representations that they, uh, the different hats that they wear.

Mm. And it will also lead you potentially to, well, what are the policy options? Because it won't be one size fits all. 'cause everybody will have different circumstances. Often all of that is conflated. It's not made clear. Um, and that's what we are trying to enable through to. Mm. It doesn't just to, just to flesh out the, the complexity of the problem.

'cause it's not simple. We've talked, talked a bit about policy here, but it also impacts upon delivery. Mm-hmm. And, and again, um, a broad example during, um, one of the various crises we had over the last, uh, five years, I was approached and asked to deliver food supply to a community for various reasons.

They, they were cut off. And the, the, the question I asked the person who was requesting it, I said, why are you asking me? And the answer given to me, and this was within the public service, was they are Maori. My, my answer to that is they are citizens.

They are citizens and citizens who do citizens get food supply from in this crisis. Now, I'm happy to lean in here, but they should expect what other citizens expect from the agency responsible for delivering that service. And then that leads to our monitoring function.

So, so in that case, sorry, I wasn't clear in that case. I think we're Trying to soak this in. Don't take the silences out. So if, if you take, take that, um, they were only seen as one thing. Yes. Um, as, as Maori and, and all of the magnificence that is being Maori, but ignoring the fact they are New Zealand citizens, and this is significant.

It wasn't deliberate. It was confused. Yeah. And it's common. It's a common confusion. Adrian killed everyone. Um, KDA, Dave, thanks for, for this, um, presentation. And thanks for the work that your whole team have done on it. Um, like a lot of us have, have been around the, um, this cope up for a long time.

And, um, and it's complex, it's difficult to get ones head around, but I just wanted to say that I thought that this was really well written. And, um, I think a lot of people who've been around it will understand it very clearly. I think Bernard used get called it a active, active learning model.

Is that Yes. Yeah. Yeah. And I, and I think, uh, for me, that's kind of like the key, um, moving forward. And from my perspective on this committee, it's not what we say today or here today. It's next year and the year after and what you report back to us.

'cause I would expect that we'll see, um, in your annual, uh, reports, um, you know, updates on how many, uh, government agencies have taken this up, what sort of, um, progress, uh, that we're making. Um, I wouldn't expect that just because we've, um, released, um, this Copa now that the switch will go on and everything will now be operating.

Um, and like it's a perfect world. Well, we all know that's not going to happen because, um, there are a whole lot of other things that government agencies are juggling. Um, and this will be just one if they, uh, agree to take it up. So, um, just want to say to all of you, um, because I think, um, it, you know, from my perspective, um, this is, um, a significant milestone in that relationship.

One well overdue. And I, I, I do remember the honorable actually talking about this. Um, and as I was reading it, I thought, oh, okay, that's what she meant. Um, so yeah, so I, I don't re I don't actually have any questions for my, my, um, is, is just encouragement.

Just get on with it. And, um, I I, yeah, I'm, I'm forever an optimist And I hope this works. There's nothing else has. Yeah. Thank you Adrian, for those, uh, those thought thoughtful comments. And if I could sort of try and get a question that's related to that, there is, uh, I, I suppose the crude way of looking at this is you've, you've created a tool, you've created the widget, now you've gotta go and sell it.

So what do you see as the challenges, uh, that, that are moving forward, uh, in, in getting this to be used widely? And, uh, how would you, uh, look at managing those challenges, taking it forward? Mm-hmm. Forward. Actually, my question is a bit like that.

So I'll just throw it in while we are, because I note in the, um, and thank you for the presentation and thank you for the incredible amount of work you've done on this. My question relates to that was that I note it says here to understand and reply to, to, uh, to essential to have a clear understanding of, um, including the understanding on the articles and the principles.

And so that is related to that as one of the challenges in the problem service, because there's two parts, aren't there? There's that part, and then there's this part that relates to it. So I guess I'm interested in that part too. Mm. I, I just start by saying, um, part of this, and Adrian has, I think, reflected on some of this is undoing learned behavior, right?

So unlearning. Yeah, unlearning. Yeah. Um, and you have to do that carefully because this isn't a framework where you can take a problem and just go bang, and it will be, an answer will pop out. You actually have to consider, because depending on the situation, you might get a different answer.

Who are you talking to in what role? So I think the first part of this would be, um, some unlearning. You have to be quite careful and, and considered in approaching this because discussions around the treaty can be quite divisive. And so we've got to be really considered on, on how we do this.

And I, and I, and I say that because if you think about that gestation, um, that Paula talked about now that went over many years, um, I, um, initiated the p the performance improvement framework in 2020. It identified we needed a policy framework, but at the time we were not responsible for what was then called the crown Maori relationship.

So this could not be moved any further than, um, so you've just gotta get the right, um, circumstances to be able to take this out. Mm-hmm. And this will be years in embed embedding. Mm. So that would, we'd need to do some unlearning. But in terms of the, the work program that we've got ahead of us all in the team have, have a plan.

We have a plan. I mean, um, uh, I think the point about it being an active learning model is the way that we want to, um, take this work forward so that, um, it how you enable, uh, environment and a culture across the public service that is that ask, what we are asking people to do is ask the questions, inquire and be considered about the problem that you're trying to resolve or the opportunity, or that you are trying to, and that's why the partnership that we have with, um, the policy project in DPMC and kawata for, uh, is important

because we are placing this within the context of policy. This, like Dave said, this is, this is policy, a policy process that's asking you to think through some very specific dimensions in applying Titi. So the learning model, uh, that idea of the implementation is quite important for us.

Um, and we will, and so part of the challenge is starting with ourselves and creating the confidence to be able to do that amongst ourselves and our own and being very diligent and deliberate, um, and disciplined actually in how we present, um, both our second opinion advice, but also our primary advice to government and making sure, um, that we are being exemplars of this work.

And then alongside that is mentioned working with agencies. We, we, we we're going with the coalition of the willing at the moment to see this as an opportunity. Well, those are important engagements because it could be well be that over the next 12 months, there might be some refinements that are made as a result of the engagements to the guidance.

So that's important. Um, but we'll also look to, they should see an improvement in potentially feedback from ministers. What, uh, might be that, um, they can see an improvement in the advice that's coming, um, through, because of the work that they're applying. So that's, you know, that that's part of the plan.

But the other part is also we do, we do a significant amount of second opinion advice and what we want to lead, what we want to move to, or what we are moving to is, for instance, if we get advice from the min, uh, requests for, um, support from the Ministry for Environment, for example, over time, we would want to be able to say back to them, these are the themes that we are seeing in terms of the advice we are providing to you, and therefore, um, if there's an opportunity to be able to work with you.

So we are not having to give back to you the things that ordinarily should be part of your policy process anyway. So it's how you kind of create a way of being able to enable people to do the thinking themselves, inquire into the thinking themselves without us necessarily having to, you know, to do that all the time. Um, so those are the, there will be challenges, no doubt about it.

Um, but what we are trying to do is be quite reasonable, reasonable and considered in, uh, moving forward and applying this, in this, applying this work and, and as I say, starting with ourselves and with those who, who want to work with us on this mahi. So you, you plan to have a, um, a dashboard of, of, or what's the method for you measuring success with this tool moving forward?

Yeah, um, we, we, we are planning to, uh, we've, we've designed a set of criteria, um, that we are currently, we've, we've kept in draft because we want to test the criteria, what success, what this would look like if we saw it in practice. And our intention is, is that by using that criteria, we would then be able to apply, um, uh, we, we would, we would be able to pro provide a status report in, in terms of, uh, a dashboard, um, for reporting.

There's two parts to it. It is the, um, it is both the, uh, the application of the ability to be able to feel comfortable in applying the, the guidance. Um, but secondly, it is then also, well, the guidance actually leading to approved outcomes for, um, for people, for the, uh, yeah, for the reason in which the policy has been designed.

So that's probably a little bit longer, that is longer term. Uh, 'cause you wanna be able to, uh, uh, apply the, it's about applying the, the work in the context of the mahi that the, that the agencies are engaged with.
